---
title: "Hawaii Wastewater Viral Activity Report"
author: "Hawaii Department of Health"
date: "2026-04-21"
output: html_document
---

```{r}
# ============================================================
# WVAL SCRIPT - SIMPLIFIED / ANNOTATED R SCRIPT
# Based on the uploaded "WVAL R Code Translation" text
#
# IMPORTANT:
# - This is plain R script format (.R), not R Markdown and not Python.
# - I added clear notes and searchable markers so your team can find 
# where to plug in jurisdiction data.
# - SEARCH FOR: TODO_HAWAII_DATA
# - SEARCH FOR: TODO_OPTIONAL_LOCAL_RULE
# - SEARCH FOR: TODO_CHECK_IF_APPLIES
#
# ============================================================


# ============================================================
# 0) LOAD LIBRARIES
# ============================================================
# These are the packages referenced in the uploaded script.
# Note:
# - data.table is used inside prioritize_sars_targets(), so it is added here
#   because the visible script uses it even though it was not loaded above.
# ============================================================

library(pacman)
p_load(
  tidyverse,
  lubridate,
  zoo,
  slider,
  stringr,
  ggplot2,
  data.table,
)
```


```{r}
# ============================================================
# 1) READ IN YOUR DATA
# ============================================================
# SEARCH KEYWORD: TODO_HAWAII_DATA
#
# Replace the example path below with your file path, OR replace the
# read_csv() line with however your team normally loads data.
#
# The goal is to end up with a data frame called: hawaii_dataset
# ============================================================

# TODO_HAWAII_DATA: ENTER YOUR DATA HERE
# Example: this will need to be changed for each person, i didn't realize it would be complicated to get a file from share point
 hawaii_dataset <- read_csv("C:/Users/jessica.freely/OneDrive - State of Hawaii/Desktop/WW Epi/DSTT Cohort 6/GitHub/WVAL_Hawaii_GitHub/contour-export-NWSS-Analyzed-CDC-Commercial-Export.csv")

# Example placeholder only:
# hawaii_dataset <- read_csv("file_path.csv")
```
#Filter out:
 
** Pcr_target_avg_conc = NA or blank omit
** Pcr_target_flowpop_lin =  NA and blank omit
 
Filter date to = "Today" Date 
```{r}
hawaii_dataset_clean <- hawaii_dataset %>%
  mutate(sample_collect_date = as.Date(sample_collect_date)) %>% 
  filter(
    !is.na(pcr_target_avg_conc) & pcr_target_avg_conc != "",
    !is.na(pcr_target_flowpop_lin) & pcr_target_flowpop_lin != "",
    sample_collect_date <= Sys.Date()
  )


```

Trouble shooting for accurate data count
```{r}
 
omitted_df <- hawaii_dataset %>%
  mutate(sample_collect_date = as.Date(sample_collect_date)) %>%
  filter(
    is.na(pcr_target_avg_conc) | pcr_target_avg_conc == "" |
    is.na(pcr_target_flowpop_lin) | pcr_target_flowpop_lin == "" |
    sample_collect_date > Sys.Date()   # future dates
  )
 
print(omitted_df)
```



```{r}
# ============================================================
# 2) PREP / STANDARDIZE YOUR JURISDICTION DATA
# ============================================================
# What this section does:
# - renames columns if your local columns do not match expected names
# - converts dates
# - lowercases pathogen fields
# - builds a same-day/site key used later for SARS target prioritization
# - keeps only WVAL respiratory targets
# - keeps the columns the downstream functions expect
#
# SEARCH KEYWORD: TODO_HAWAII_DATA
# This is the main place your team will likely edit first.
# ============================================================

data <- hawaii_dataset_clean %>%

  # ------------------------------------------------------------
  # TODO_HAWAII_DATA:
  # If your columns already match the names used below, leave this OFF.
  # If your columns have different names, uncomment and edit this block.
  # ------------------------------------------------------------
  # rename(
  #   site_id = sample_site_id,
  #   source = funding,
  #   lab_id = micro_lab_name,
  #   wwtp_name = sample_site_name,
  #   population_served = totalpop,
  #   county_names = county
  # ) %>%

  # Convert sample collection date into an R Date object.
  # The original script allows both month-day-year and year-month-day style.
  # Example entered: "04/15/2024"
  # Need: "2024-04-15"
  mutate(
    sample_collect_date = as.Date(
      lubridate::parse_date_time(sample_collect_date, orders = c("mdY", "Ymd"))
    )
  ) %>%

  # Lowercase pathogen names and gene target names
  # so matching later is consistent.
  mutate(
    pcr_target = tolower(pcr_target),
  #  pcr_gene_target = tolower(pcr_gene_target)
  ) %>%

  # Build a same-site / same-day key used by the SARS gene-target logic.
  # Example "Hawaii_WWTP1_wwtp_na_wastewater_sars-cov-2_2024-04-01"
  mutate(
    key_plot_with_pcr_by_day = paste(
      wwtp_jurisdiction,
      wwtp_name,
      sample_location,
      sample_location_specify,
      sample_matrix,
      pcr_target,
      sample_collect_date,
      sep = "_"
    )
  ) %>%

  # Keep only the respiratory targets supported by this WVAL method.
  filter(pcr_target %in% c("fluav", "rsv", "sars-cov-2")) %>%

  # Keep the fields the downstream code expects.
  select(
    wwtp_jurisdiction,
    source,
    lab_id,
    site_id,
    wwtp_name,
    population_served,
    sample_id,
    sample_collect_date,
    pcr_target,
    # pcr_gene_target,
    pcr_target_avg_conc,
    pcr_target_flowpop_lin, #normalize by flow rate and population
    lod_sewage,
    county_names,
    concentration_method,
    extraction_method,
    major_lab_method,
    key_plot_with_pcr_by_day,
  ) %>%

  # Drop rows missing concentration.
  filter(!is.na(pcr_target_avg_conc))
```


```{r}
# ============================================================
# 3) SARS-CoV-2 SPECIAL HANDLING **we dont need step 3 we have just one gene target (n) for sars-cov-2
# ============================================================
# What this section does:
# A) If multiple SARS gene targets exist for the same site/day,
#    keep the preferred one using CDC-style ranking.
# B) If both N1 and N2 exist for the same sample_id,
#    combine them using a geometric mean.
#
# These rules are only needed if your jurisdiction actually has
# multiple SARS targets or paired N1/N2 data.
# ============================================================


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 3A) PRIORITIZE SARS GENE TARGETS
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# Plain English:
# If one site/day has multiple SARS gene targets, keep the preferred one.
# The ranking in the original script is:
#   1 = N targets
#   2 = ORF1
#   3 = E
#   4 = RdRP
#   5 = S
#   6 = TaqPath S
#  99 = anything else / unrecognized
#
# SEARCH KEYWORD: TODO_CHECK_IF_APPLIES
# ------------------------------------------------------------

# prioritize_sars_targets <- function(data) {

 # dt <- data.table(data)

 # non_sarscov2_dt <- dt[pcr_target != "sars-cov-2", ]
 # sarscov2_dt     <- dt[pcr_target == "sars-cov-2", ]

  # Assign a numeric rank to each SARS target.
 # sarscov2_dt[, gene_rank := fcase(
   #pcr_gene_target == "n", 1,
  #grepl("n1|n2|n3|n1 and n2 combined|ddcov_n", pcr_gene_target, ignore.case = TRUE), 1,
  #grepl("orf1", pcr_gene_target, ignore.case = TRUE), 2,
   #grepl("e_sarbeco|ddcov_e", pcr_gene_target, ignore.case = TRUE), 3,
    #grepl("rdrp", pcr_gene_target, ignore.case = TRUE), 4,
    #pcr_gene_target == "s", 5,
    #grepl("taqpath s", pcr_gene_target, ignore.case = TRUE), 6,
    #default = 99
  #)]

  # For each site/day key, keep the lowest (best) rank.
  #sarscov2_dt <- sarscov2_dt[
   # , .SD[gene_rank == min(gene_rank, na.rm = TRUE)],
    #by = .(key_plot_with_pcr_by_day)]

  #sarscov2_dt[, gene_rank := NULL]

  #data <- as.data.frame(rbind(non_sarscov2_dt, sarscov2_dt))
  #return(data)}


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 3B) COMBINE N1 / N2 INTO ONE SARS VALUE
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# Plain English:
# If the same sample_id has both N1 and N2, combine them into one row.
# If at least one is above the LOD, use the geometric mean of the
# concentrations. Otherwise, use LOD/2.
#
# SEARCH KEYWORD: TODO_CHECK_IF_APPLIES
# ------------------------------------------------------------

#calc_n1n2_avg <- function(data) {

  #samples_with_both <- data %>%
    #filter(
      #pcr_target == "sars-cov-2",
      #pcr_gene_target %in% c("n1", "n2")
    #) %>%
    #group_by(sample_id) %>%
    #summarise(n_gene_targets = n_distinct(pcr_gene_target), .groups = "drop") %>%
    #filter(n_gene_targets == 2) %>%
    #pull(sample_id)

  #sars_averaged <- data %>%
    #filter(sample_id %in% samples_with_both) %>%
    #group_by(sample_id) %>%
    #reframe(
      #avg_lod = mean(lod_sewage, na.rm = TRUE),
      #n_above_lod = sum(pcr_target_avg_conc >= lod_sewage, na.rm = TRUE),
      #pcr_target_avg_conc = if_else(
        #n_above_lod > 0,
        #10^(mean(log10(pcr_target_avg_conc), na.rm = TRUE)),
        #avg_lod / 2
      #),
      #lod_sewage = avg_lod,
      #pcr_gene_target_agg = "n2 and n1"
    #) %>%
    #ungroup() %>%
    #distinct(sample_id, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
    #select(-avg_lod, -n_above_lod)

  #sars_individual <- data %>%
    #filter(!sample_id %in% samples_with_both) %>%
    #mutate(pcr_gene_target_agg = pcr_gene_target)

  #final_result <- bind_rows(sars_averaged, sars_individual)

  #return(final_result)}


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# APPLY SARS-SPECIFIC FUNCTIONS
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# TODO_CHECK_IF_APPLIES:
# - If your jurisdiction never had multiple SARS gene targets,
#   you may not need prioritize_sars_targets().
# - If your jurisdiction does not have paired N1/N2 sample rows,
#   you may not need calc_n1n2_avg().
# ------------------------------------------------------------

#data <- data %>%
 # prioritize_sars_targets() %>%
  #calc_n1n2_avg()
```


```{r}
# ============================================================
# 4) HELPER FUNCTIONS
# ============================================================
# These are small utility functions used later by the WVAL logic.
# ============================================================


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 4A) APPROXIMATE QUANTILE
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# Used to imitate PySpark-style quantile behavior.
# what the function does quantile_approx(x, p)
#   Give me the value at the p-th percentile of this data
# PySpark does not interpolate the same way R does
# ------------------------------------------------------------

quantile_approx <- function(x, p) {
  x <- sort(x) #sort the data 

  if (!length(x) || all(is.na(x))) return(NA_real_) #handle edge cases, if data is empty return NA 
  if (p >= 1) return(x[length(x)]) #if asking for the 100th percentile, return max value 

  i_x <- p * (length(x) - 1) + 1 #find position in the sorted list where the percentile sits
  x[floor(i_x)] #round down and grab that lower index value 
}


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 4B) DAYS TO RECALCULATION TARGET DATES
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# SARS-CoV-2 baseline recalculates around:
#   - April 1
#   - October 1
#
# Flu A / RSV baseline recalculates around:
#   - August 1
# 
# ------------------------------------------------------------

calc_days_to_target_sars <- function(date) {
  date    <- as.Date(date)
  y       <- year(date)
  april_1 <- as.Date(sprintf("%d-04-01", y))
  oct_1   <- as.Date(sprintf("%d-10-01", y))
  m       <- month(date)

# How many days is this sample date from the nearest baseline recalculation date?
  
  if (m >= 4 && m <= 9) {    #Is the month between April (4) and September (9)?
    abs(as.integer(difftime(date, april_1, units = "days"))) #how many days from April 1
  } else {
    abs(as.integer(difftime(date, oct_1, units = "days"))) #how many days from Oct  1
  }
}

calc_days_to_target_flu_rsv <- function(date) {
  date <- as.Date(date)
  y    <- year(date)
  aug1 <- as.Date(sprintf("%d-08-01", y))
  augN <- as.Date(sprintf("%d-08-01", y + 1))

  pmin(
    abs(as.integer(difftime(date, aug1, units = "days"))),
    abs(as.integer(difftime(date, augN, units = "days")))
  )
}


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 4C) OPTIONAL PLOT KEY HELPER
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# Function for creating key for plots
# Use it only if it helps your local workflow.
# ------------------------------------------------------------

make_key_plot_with_pcr <- function(df) {
  kp <- paste(
    coalesce(tolower(df$wwtp_jurisdiction), ""),
    coalesce(tolower(df$wwtp_name), ""),
    coalesce(df$sample_location, ""),
    coalesce(df$sample_matrix, ""),
    coalesce(as.character(df$major_lab_method), ""),
    sep = "-"
  )

  kp <- str_replace_all(kp, "-{2,}", "-")
  kp <- str_replace_all(kp, "^-+|-+$", "")

  kp
}


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 4D) REMOVE OUTLIERS
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# The script removes extreme outliers using z-score > 4 within each
# site/pathogen/source/method combination.
# ------------------------------------------------------------

remove_outliers <- function(data) {
  # calculate mean and standard deviation for each site_lab_norm combo
  mean_std_ln <- data %>%
    group_by(site_id_with_pcr_source_mlm) %>%
    summarise(
      mean_avg_conc_ln = mean(pcr_target_avg_conc_ln, na.rm = TRUE),
      stddev_avg_conc_ln = sd(pcr_target_avg_conc_ln, na.rm = TRUE)) 
  
  clean_df <- left_join(data, mean_std_ln, by = "site_id_with_pcr_source_mlm")
  
  # set z-score threshold
  z_score_threshold <- 4
  
  # calculate and filter the Z-score, handling NA stddev and division by zero
  clean_df <- clean_df %>%
    mutate(z_score = ifelse((is.na(stddev_avg_conc_ln) | stddev_avg_conc_ln == 0), 0, # assign a z-score to ensure rows won't be removed
                            abs(pcr_target_avg_conc_ln - mean_avg_conc_ln) / stddev_avg_conc_ln))
  
  # store outliers
  outliers <- clean_df %>%
    filter(z_score > z_score_threshold)
  
  # filter out outliers based on the z-score threshold
  clean_df <- clean_df %>% 
    filter((z_score <= z_score_threshold) | is.na(z_score)) %>%
    select(-mean_avg_conc_ln, -stddev_avg_conc_ln, -z_score)
  return(clean_df)
}


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 4E) TURN NUMERIC WVAL INTO CATEGORY LABELS
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# This function converts numeric wastewater viral activity (WVAL) values into 
# categorical levels—Very Low, Low, Moderate, High, or Very High—based on predefined 
# thresholds. 
# 
# The cutoff ranges differ for SARS-CoV-2, Influenza A, and RSV, with each pathogen 
# having its own scale for assigning these categories.
# ------------------------------------------------------------

categorize_wval <- function(ww_index, target) {
  t   <- tolower(target)
  out <- rep(NA_character_, length(ww_index))

  is_sars <- stringr::str_detect(t, "sars")
  is_flu  <- stringr::str_detect(t, "fluav")
  is_rsv  <- stringr::str_detect(t, "rsv")

  out[is_sars] <- as.character(cut(
    ww_index[is_sars],
    breaks = c(-Inf, 2.0, 3.4, 5.3, 7.8, Inf),
    labels = c("Very Low", "Low", "Moderate", "High", "Very High"),
    right  = TRUE
  ))

  out[is_flu] <- as.character(cut(
    ww_index[is_flu],
    breaks = c(-Inf, 2.7, 6.2, 11.2, 17.6, Inf),
    labels = c("Very Low", "Low", "Moderate", "High", "Very High"),
    right  = TRUE
  ))

  out[is_rsv] <- as.character(cut(
    ww_index[is_rsv],
    breaks = c(-Inf, 2.5, 5.2, 8, 11, Inf),
    labels = c("Very Low", "Low", "Moderate", "High", "Very High"),
    right  = TRUE
  ))

  out
}
```


```{r}
# ============================================================
# 5) MAIN WVAL FUNCTION
# ============================================================
# What this function does:
# 1) filters to one pathogen
# 2) handles below-LOD values
# 3) creates log transforms
# 4) builds site/pathogen/source/method IDs
# 5) adjusts for long gaps in method history
# 6) removes outliers
# 7) calculates distance to baseline recalculation dates
# 8) calculates baseline and standard deviation
# 9) calculates wastewater viral activity index values
# 10) handles overlapping major lab methods
# 11) assigns WVAL categories
# 12) aggregates results to the site-week level
#
# Final output:
# One row per site per week with:
# - numeric weekly WVAL value
# - weekly WVAL category
# ============================================================

wval_v2 <- function(data, target) {
  
  # =========================================================
  # STEP 1: FILTER + CLEAN CONCENTRATIONS
  # =========================================================
  # Goal:
  # - Keep only one pathogen (SARS, Flu, or RSV)
  # - Fix values below detection limit (LOD)
  # - Create log versions for later math
  #
  # WHY:
  # WVAL is based on log-transformed data and requires
  # consistent handling of low/undetected values
  # =========================================================
  
  all_final <- data %>%
    filter(pcr_target == target & population_served >= 3000) %>%
    mutate(
      # Mark if value is below detection limit
      pcr_target_below_lod = if_else(pcr_target_avg_conc < lod_sewage, "yes", "no"),
      
      # Mark if detected
      pcr_target_detect    = if_else(pcr_target_avg_conc < lod_sewage, "no", "yes"),
      
      # Replace below-LOD values with LOD/2 (standard practice)
      pcr_target_avg_conc_lin = if_else(
        pcr_target_below_lod == "yes",
        lod_sewage / 2,
        pcr_target_avg_conc
      ),
      
      # Log10 version (used in some calculations)
      pcr_target_avg_conc_log10 = if_else(
        pcr_target_below_lod == "yes",
        log10(lod_sewage / 2),
        log10(pcr_target_avg_conc_lin)
      ),
      
      # Natural log version (used for WVAL math)
      pcr_target_avg_conc_ln = log(pcr_target_avg_conc_lin),
      
      # Extract year from date
      year = year(sample_collect_date)
    )
  
  
  # =========================================================
  # STEP 2: CREATE "SERIES" (SITE + METHOD GROUPINGS)
  # =========================================================
  # Goal:
  # - Define what counts as one continuous dataset
  # - Handle changes in lab methods over time
  #
  # WHY:
  # WVAL must be calculated separately for each:
  #   site + pathogen + source + lab method
  # =========================================================
  
  all_final1 <- all_final %>%
    
    # OPTIONAL FILTERS (only if your jurisdiction uses them)
    # TODO_OPTIONAL_LOCAL_RULE
    
    group_by(site_id, source, major_lab_method) %>%
    arrange(desc(sample_collect_date), .by_group = TRUE) %>%
    
    # --------------------------------------------------------
  # Handle long gaps (> 365 days)
  #
  # If there's a big gap in data, treat it like a new "era"
  # by slightly changing the lab method value (e.g., +0.1)
  #
  # WHY:
  # Prevents mixing old and new data into one baseline
  # --------------------------------------------------------
  mutate(
    sample_collect_date = as.Date(sample_collect_date),
    date_diff = as.numeric(abs(sample_collect_date - lag(sample_collect_date))),
    is_gap_start = case_when(
      is.na(date_diff)   ~ 0,
      date_diff <= 365   ~ 0,
      date_diff > 365    ~ 1
    ),
    cumsum_gaps = cumsum(na.omit(is_gap_start)),
    major_lab_method = as.numeric(major_lab_method),
    mlm_increment = if_else(cumsum_gaps > 0, cumsum_gaps * 0.1, 0),
    major_lab_method = if_else(
      cumsum_gaps > 0,
      major_lab_method + mlm_increment,
      major_lab_method
    )
  ) %>%
    
    # Clean up helper columns
    ungroup() %>%
    select(-date_diff, -is_gap_start, -cumsum_gaps, -mlm_increment) %>%
    
    # Drop missing key fields
    drop_na(site_id) %>%
    drop_na(sample_collect_date) %>%
    drop_na(population_served) %>%
    
    # --------------------------------------------------------
  # Create unique IDs for grouping
  #
  # These define a "data series" for WVAL calculations
  # --------------------------------------------------------
  mutate(
    site_id_with_pcr            = paste(site_id, pcr_target, sep = "_"),
    site_id_with_pcr_source     = paste(site_id, pcr_target, source, sep = "_"),
    site_id_with_pcr_source_mlm = paste(site_id_with_pcr, source, major_lab_method, sep = "_")
  ) %>%
    
    # OPTIONAL: some jurisdictions group here
    # TODO_OPTIONAL_LOCAL_RULE
    
    # Calculate time since first sample
    mutate(
      days_since_first_sample = as.integer(
        difftime(sample_collect_date, min(sample_collect_date), units = "days")
      ),
      first_collection_date = min(sample_collect_date)
    ) %>%
    ungroup()
  
  
  # =========================================================
  # STEP 3: REMOVE OUTLIERS
  # =========================================================
  # Goal:
  # Remove extreme values that could distort baseline
  #
  # HOW:
  # Uses z-score > 4 threshold within each series
  # =========================================================
  
  all_final2 <- remove_outliers(data = all_final1)
  
  
  # =========================================================
  # STEP 4: ALIGN DATA TO BASELINE RECALCULATION DATES
  # =========================================================
  # Goal:
  # Calculate how far each sample is from the nearest
  # baseline recalculation date
  #
  # WHY:
  # CDC recalculates baselines on fixed dates:
  #   SARS: April 1 and October 1
  #   Flu/RSV: August 1
  # =========================================================
  
  if (target == "sars-cov-2") {
    
    all_final3 <- all_final2 %>%
      
      # Fill in missing dates so time calculations work
      arrange(sample_collect_date, .by_group = TRUE) %>%
      complete(
        sample_collect_date = seq.Date(
          min(sample_collect_date, na.rm = TRUE),
          max(sample_collect_date, na.rm = TRUE),
          by = "day"
        )
      ) %>%
      
      mutate(
        # Split year into two halves:
        # Apr–Sep vs Oct–Mar
        half_year = if_else(month(sample_collect_date) >= 4 & month(sample_collect_date) <= 9, 1, 2),
        
        year = year(sample_collect_date),
        
        # Calculate distance to nearest April 1 or October 1
        days_to_nearest_target = sapply(sample_collect_date, calc_days_to_target_sars)
      ) %>%
      
      # Find closest samples to recalculation dates
      group_by(site_id_with_pcr_source_mlm, half_year, year) %>%
      mutate(min_days_to_target = min(days_to_nearest_target, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
      ungroup() %>%
      select(-half_year)
    
  } else {
    
    all_final3 <- all_final2 %>%
      arrange(sample_collect_date, .by_group = TRUE) %>%
      complete(
        sample_collect_date = seq.Date(
          min(sample_collect_date, na.rm = TRUE),
          max(sample_collect_date, na.rm = TRUE),
          by = "day"
        )
      ) %>%
      
      mutate(
        year_val = year(sample_collect_date),
        
        # Distance to August 1
        days_to_nearest_target = sapply(sample_collect_date, calc_days_to_target_flu_rsv)
      ) %>%
      
      group_by(site_id_with_pcr_source_mlm, year_val) %>%
      mutate(min_days_to_target = min(days_to_nearest_target, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
      ungroup()
      } 

    # ============================================================
    # STEP 5: CALCULATE BASELINE + STANDARD DEVIATION
    # ============================================================
    # Goal:
    # - For each site/pathogen/source/method series, calculate the
    #   reference baseline and standard deviation used for WVAL
    #
    # WHY:
    # WVAL compares each sample to a site-specific "normal low level"
    # using:
    #   baseline = 10th percentile of recent log concentrations
    #   std dev  = spread of recent log concentrations
    #
    # RULES:
    # - SARS-CoV-2:
    #     use first 6 months OR recalculation points nearest Apr 1 / Oct 1
    # - Flu A / RSV:
    #     use first 12 months OR recalculation points nearest Aug 1
    #
    # The rolling window here is 730 days = 24 months
    # ============================================================
    
    if(target == "sars-cov-2") {
      all_final4 <- all_final3 %>%
        group_by(site_id_with_pcr_source_mlm) %>%
        mutate(
          # baseline_flag marks rows where baseline should be recalculated:
          # - during the first 6 months of data, OR
          # - at the sample(s) closest to the Apr/Oct recalculation date
          baseline_flag = (days_since_first_sample <= 182) |
            (days_to_nearest_target == min_days_to_target),
          
          # temp_baseline:
          # compute the rolling 10th percentile of ln concentration
          # over the prior 24 months, but only on rows where baseline_flag is TRUE
          temp_baseline = if_else(
            baseline_flag,
            slide_index_dbl(
              pcr_target_avg_conc_ln,
              sample_collect_date,
              .before = 730, .after = 0, .complete = FALSE,
              .f = ~ quantile_approx(.x, 0.10)
            ),
            NA_real_
          ),
          
          # Carry the most recent baseline forward until the next recalculation
          baseline_avg_conc_ln = na.locf(temp_baseline, na.rm = FALSE),
          
          # temp_std:
          # rolling standard deviation over the same 24-month window
          temp_std = if_else(
            baseline_flag,
            slide_index_dbl(
              pcr_target_avg_conc_ln,
              sample_collect_date,
              .before = 730, .after = 0, .complete = FALSE,
              .f = ~ sd(.x, na.rm = TRUE)
            ),
            NA_real_
          ),
          
          # Carry the most recent std dev forward too
          std_avg_conc_ln = na.locf(temp_std, na.rm = FALSE)
        ) %>%
        select(-c(temp_std, temp_baseline, baseline_flag)) %>%
        drop_na(pcr_target) %>%
        ungroup()
      
    } else {
      
      all_final4 <- all_final3 %>%
        group_by(site_id_with_pcr_source_mlm) %>%
        mutate(
          # For Flu/RSV, baseline recalculates:
          # - during the first 12 months, OR
          # - at the sample(s) closest to Aug 1
          # Also limited here to months Aug-Dec for the annual reset logic
          baseline_flag = (days_since_first_sample <= 365) |
            (days_to_nearest_target == min_days_to_target) &
            (month(sample_collect_date) %in% 8:12),
          
          temp_baseline = if_else(
            baseline_flag,
            slide_index_dbl(
              pcr_target_avg_conc_ln,
              sample_collect_date,
              .before = 730, .after = 0, .complete = FALSE,
              .f = ~ quantile_approx(.x, 0.10)
            ),
            NA_real_
          ),
          
          # Carry the last calculated baseline forward
          baseline_avg_conc_ln = na.locf(temp_baseline, na.rm = FALSE),
          
          temp_std = if_else(
            baseline_flag,
            slide_index_dbl(
              pcr_target_avg_conc_ln,
              sample_collect_date,
              .before = 730, .after = 0, .complete = FALSE,
              .f = ~ sd(.x, na.rm = TRUE)
            ),
            NA_real_
          ),
          
          # Carry the last calculated std dev forward
          std_avg_conc_ln = na.locf(temp_std, na.rm = FALSE)
        ) %>%
        select(-temp_baseline, -temp_std, -baseline_flag) %>%
        drop_na(pcr_target) %>%
        ungroup()
    }
    
    
    # ============================================================
    # STEP 6: SAVE THE LAST VALID BASELINE + STANDARD DEVIATION
    # ============================================================
    # Goal:
    # For each site/pathogen/source/method series, keep the most recent
    # non-missing baseline and std dev values.
    #
    # WHY:
    # Those "last" values are what each sample will be compared against
    # to calculate its WVAL score.
    # ============================================================
    
    last_vals <- all_final4 %>%
      filter(!is.na(std_avg_conc_ln) & !is.na(baseline_avg_conc_ln)) %>%
      group_by(site_id_with_pcr_source_mlm) %>%
      slice_tail(n = 1) %>%
      summarise(
        last_baseline_ln = baseline_avg_conc_ln,
        last_std_ln = std_avg_conc_ln
      )
    
    
    # ============================================================
    # STEP 7: CALCULATE WASTEWATER INDEX (WVAL SCORE)
    # ============================================================
    # Goal:
    # Convert each sample’s concentration into a standardized score
    # relative to its site's baseline.
    #
    # FORMULA:
    #   ww_index_ln = (sample_ln - baseline_ln) / std_ln
    #   ww_index_ln_lin = exp(ww_index_ln)
    #
    # WHY:
    # - The first formula says how many standard deviations above/below
    #   baseline the sample is
    # - exp() converts that back to a positive linear scale used for WVAL
    #
    # Sites with less than 56 days of data are excluded here
    # ============================================================
    
    result <- all_final4 %>%
      left_join(last_vals, by = "site_id_with_pcr_source_mlm") %>%
      group_by(site_id_with_pcr_source_mlm) %>%
      mutate(
        # Number of days this site/method/pathogen series has existed
        days_site_online = max(days_since_first_sample, na.rm = TRUE)
      ) %>%
      ungroup() %>%
      mutate(
        # Do not calculate WVAL until there are at least 56 days of data
        ww_index_ln = case_when(
          days_site_online < 56 ~ NA_real_,
          TRUE ~ (pcr_target_avg_conc_ln - last_baseline_ln) / last_std_ln
        ),
        
        # Convert back to linear scale
        ww_index_ln_lin = exp(ww_index_ln),
        
        # Create week-ending date for weekly aggregation
        day_of_week = wday(sample_collect_date),
        week_end = sample_collect_date + days(7 - day_of_week)
      ) %>%
      group_by(site_id_with_pcr_source_mlm, week_end) %>%
      arrange(sample_collect_date, .by_group = TRUE) %>%
      ungroup() %>%
      distinct()
    
    # Save an intermediate copy for troubleshooting if needed
    intermediate_result <<- result
    
    
    # ============================================================
    # STEP 8: HANDLE OVERLAPPING MAJOR LAB METHODS (MLMs)
    # ============================================================
    # Goal:
    # If the same site has overlapping method eras in the same week,
    # keep the most recent one.
    #
    # WHY:
    # Avoid double-counting a site in weeks where old and new lab-method
    # series overlap.
    # ============================================================
    
    result <- result %>%
      group_by(site_id_with_pcr_source_mlm) %>%
      mutate(
        # Earliest date this MLM series appears
        min_sample_date_for_mlm = min(sample_collect_date, na.rm = TRUE)
      ) %>%
      ungroup() %>%
      
      # Within each site/week, rank the series so the most recent MLM comes first
      group_by(site_id_with_pcr, week_end) %>%
      arrange(desc(min_sample_date_for_mlm), desc(major_lab_method)) %>%
      mutate(
        latest_min_date_rank = rank(
          -as.numeric(min_sample_date_for_mlm),
          ties.method = "min"
        )
      ) %>%
      ungroup() %>%
      
      # Keep only the newest non-missing WVAL series for that site/week
      filter(latest_min_date_rank == 1, !is.na(ww_index_ln_lin))
    
    
    # ============================================================
    # STEP 9: ASSIGN WVAL CATEGORY TO EACH RESULT
    # ============================================================
    # Goal:
    # Turn the numeric WVAL score into:
    #   Very Low / Low / Moderate / High / Very High
    # based on pathogen-specific cutoffs
    # ============================================================
    
    unaggregated_result <- result %>%
      mutate(wval_result = categorize_wval(ww_index_ln_lin, pcr_target)) %>%
      distinct()
    
    # Save unaggregated copy for troubleshooting if needed
    unaggregated_result <<- unaggregated_result
    
    
    # ============================================================
    # STEP 10: CALCULATE SITE-WEEKLY AVERAGE WVAL
    # ============================================================
    # Goal:
    # Collapse multiple samples within the same site/week into a single
    # weekly WVAL value for that site.
    #
    # HOW:
    # - average the weekly WVAL values within each site/week
    # - keep selected metadata fields
    # - assign the weekly category label
    # ============================================================
    
    site_weekly_average <- function(data) {
      site_ag <- data %>%
        filter(!is.na(ww_index_ln_lin)) %>%
        arrange(desc(sample_collect_date)) %>%
        group_by(site_id_with_pcr, week_end) %>%
        
        reframe(
          site_id = first(site_id),
          mean_pcr_target_avg_conc_lin = mean(pcr_target_avg_conc_lin, na.rm = TRUE),
          all_pcr_target_avg_conc_lin = paste(pcr_target_avg_conc_lin, collapse = ", "),
          wval_site_wk = mean(ww_index_ln_lin, na.rm = TRUE),
          all_sample_collect_dates = paste(sample_collect_date, collapse = ", "),
          days_since_first_sample = last(days_since_first_sample),
          baseline_avg_conc_ln = last(baseline_avg_conc_ln),
          last_baseline_ln = last(last_baseline_ln),
          last_std_ln = last(last_std_ln),
          std_avg_conc_ln = last(std_avg_conc_ln),
          pcr_target_below_lod = first(pcr_target_below_lod),
          pcr_target = first(pcr_target),
          all_ww_index_ln_lin = paste(ww_index_ln_lin, collapse = ", "),
          # all_sample_ids = paste(sample_id, collapse = ", "),
          source = source,
          county_names = county_names
        ) %>%
        
        # Convert numeric weekly WVAL into category label
        mutate(
          wval_site_wk_level = categorize_wval(wval_site_wk, pcr_target)
        ) %>%
        distinct()
      
      return(site_ag)
    }
    
    
    # ============================================================
    # STEP 11: RETURN SITE-WEEK RESULTS
    # ============================================================
    # This is the final output of wval_v2():
    # one row per site per week with numeric WVAL + category
    # ============================================================
    
    site_ag <- site_weekly_average(unaggregated_result)
    
    return(site_ag)
    
  }
  
  
  # ============================================================
  # Step: 12 COUNTY-LEVEL AGGREGATION FUNCTION
  # ============================================================
  # Goal:
  # Aggregate site-week WVAL values up to the county level.
  #
  # HOW:
  # - take the median site-week WVAL within each county/week/pathogen
  # - assign a category using the same cutoff function
  # ============================================================
  
  wval_county_wk_agg <- function(data) {
    
    county_ag <- data %>%
      group_by(county_names, week_end, pcr_target) %>%
      reframe(
        week_end = first(week_end),
        wval_county_wk = median(wval_site_wk)
      ) %>%
      unique() %>%
      arrange(county_names, week_end) %>%
      ungroup()
    
    county_ag <- county_ag %>%
      mutate(
        wval_county_wk_level = categorize_wval(wval_county_wk, pcr_target)
      )
    
    return(county_ag)
  }

# ============================================================
# STEP 13: CHECK REQUIRED COLUMNS (RUN THIS FIRST)
# ============================================================
# Goal:
# Make sure your Hawaii dataset has all required columns
# BEFORE running any processing.
#
# SEARCH KEYWORD: TODO_HAWAII_DATA
# ============================================================

required_columns <- c(
  "wwtp_jurisdiction",
  "source",
  "lab_id",
  "site_id",
  "wwtp_name",
  "population_served",
  "sample_id",
  "sample_collect_date",
  "pcr_target",
  # "pcr_gene_target",
  "pcr_target_avg_conc",
  "pcr_target_flowpop_lin",
  "lod_sewage",
  "county_names",
  "concentration_method",
  "extraction_method",
  "major_lab_method",
  "sample_location",
  "sample_location_specify",
  "sample_matrix"
)

# Run this after loading hawaii_dataset:
# missing_columns <- setdiff(required_columns, names(hawaii_dataset))
# print(missing_columns)

# If anything prints, you are missing required fields.


# ============================================================
# STEP 14: IDENTIFY WHERE TO EDIT THE SCRIPT
# ============================================================
# Goal:
# Show your team the main places in the script they may need to update
# for Hawaii-specific data and logic.
#
# SEARCH FOR:
# - TODO_HAWAII_DATA
#     -> load your data + rename columns
# - TODO_OPTIONAL_LOCAL_RULE
#     -> optional filtering or grouping rules
# - TODO_CHECK_IF_APPLIES
#     -> SARS-specific logic that may or may not apply
# ============================================================

















# ============================================================
# STEP 15: RUN WVAL FUNCTIONS
# ============================================================
# Goal:
# Run the main WVAL function for each pathogen and then create
# county-level results from the site-level outputs.
#
# Output:
# - site_ag_*   = site-week WVAL results
# - county_ag_* = county-week WVAL results
#
# NOTE:
# Only run this AFTER:
# - data loads successfully
# - column check passes
# - prep steps run without error
# ============================================================

#######################################
## Call site and county WVAL functions
#######################################

site_ag_sars <- wval_v2(data, target = "sars-cov-2")
site_ag_flu  <- wval_v2(data, target = "fluav")
site_ag_rsv  <- wval_v2(data, target = "rsv")

county_ag_sars <- wval_county_wk_agg(data = site_ag_sars)
county_ag_flu  <- wval_county_wk_agg(data = site_ag_flu)
# Add this if you also want county-level RSV output:
# county_ag_rsv <- wval_county_wk_agg(data = site_ag_rsv)


# ============================================================
# STEP 16: CREATE STATE-LEVEL WVAL
# ============================================================
# Goal:
# Aggregate site-level weekly WVAL values up to the state level.
#
# HOW:
# - Take the median site-week WVAL across all sites in a week
# - Assign a WVAL category using the same pathogen-specific cutoffs
#
# Output:
# - One row per week with:
#     state_wval  = numeric state-level WVAL
#     state_level = categorical level
# ============================================================

#######################################
## Create State WVAL
#######################################

state_wval_sarscov2 <- site_ag_sars %>%
  group_by(week_end) %>%
  mutate(state_wval = median(wval_site_wk, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  mutate(state_level = categorize_wval(state_wval, pcr_target)) %>%
  distinct(week_end, state_wval, state_level)

state_wval_flu <- site_ag_flu %>%
  group_by(week_end) %>%
  mutate(state_wval = median(wval_site_wk, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  mutate(state_level = categorize_wval(state_wval, pcr_target)) %>%
  distinct(week_end, state_wval, state_level)

state_wval_rsv <- site_ag_rsv %>%
  group_by(week_end) %>%
  mutate(state_wval = median(wval_site_wk, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  mutate(state_level = categorize_wval(state_wval, pcr_target)) %>%
  distinct(week_end, state_wval, state_level)


# ============================================================
# STEP 17: DEFINE CDC COMPARISON / CONCORDANCE FUNCTION
# ============================================================
# Goal:
# Compare Hawaii's state-level WVAL categories to CDC NWSS categories
# and summarize how often they agree.
#
# Output:
# - total number of observations compared
# - number and percent concordant
# - number and percent discordant
# - contingency table of category matches/mismatches
# - discordance distance (how far apart mismatched categories are)
#
# IMPORTANT:
# Inputs must already be aligned so that the two vectors represent
# the same weeks in the same order.
# ============================================================

compare_wval_function <- function(state_wval, nwss_wval, print_matches = TRUE) {
  
  # ----------------------------------------------------------
  # Convert WVAL values into ordered categories
  # ----------------------------------------------------------
  # This ensures the levels have a meaningful order:
  # Very Low < Low < Moderate < High < Very High
  # ----------------------------------------------------------
  state_wval <- factor(
    state_wval,
    levels = c("Very Low", "Low", "Moderate", "High", "Very High"),
    ordered = TRUE
  )
  
  nwss_wval <- factor(
    nwss_wval,
    levels = c("Very Low", "Low", "Moderate", "High", "Very High"),
    ordered = TRUE
  )
  
  # ----------------------------------------------------------
  # Check that both inputs have the same length
  # ----------------------------------------------------------
  if (length(state_wval) != length(nwss_wval)) {
    stop("Variables must have the same length")
  }
  
  # ----------------------------------------------------------
  # Create row-by-row comparison table
  # ----------------------------------------------------------
  comparison_df <- data.frame(
    state_wval = state_wval,
    nwss_wval = nwss_wval,
    Match = state_wval == nwss_wval
  )
  
  # ----------------------------------------------------------
  # Calculate summary statistics
  # ----------------------------------------------------------
  total_obs <- nrow(comparison_df)
  matching_obs <- sum(comparison_df$Match)
  non_matching_obs <- total_obs - matching_obs
  match_percentage <- (matching_obs / total_obs) * 100
  discordance_percentage <- (non_matching_obs / total_obs) * 100
  
  # ----------------------------------------------------------
  # Identify where discordance happens
  # ----------------------------------------------------------
  discordance_levels <- table(
    state_wval[!comparison_df$Match],
    nwss_wval[!comparison_df$Match]
  )
  
  # ----------------------------------------------------------
  # Print high-level summary
  # ----------------------------------------------------------
  cat("Concordance Analysis:\n")
  cat("Total observations:", total_obs, "\n")
  cat("Concordant observations:", matching_obs, "\n")
  cat("Discordant observations:", non_matching_obs, "\n")
  cat("Concordance percentage:", round(match_percentage, 2), "%\n")
  cat("Discordance percentage:", round(discordance_percentage, 2), "%\n\n")
  
  # ----------------------------------------------------------
  # Print full contingency table
  # ----------------------------------------------------------
  cont_table <- table(state_wval, nwss_wval)
  cat("Contingency Table (Counts):\n")
  print(cont_table)
  
  # ----------------------------------------------------------
  # If mismatches exist, describe them in more detail
  # ----------------------------------------------------------
  if (non_matching_obs > 0) {
    cat("\nDiscordance Details:\n")
    cat("Observations where categorizations differ:\n")
    print(discordance_levels)
    
    cat("\nDetailed Discordance Analysis:\n")
    
    # Distance = how many category levels apart the mismatch is
    discordance_distance <- sapply(which(!comparison_df$Match), function(i) {
      abs(as.numeric(state_wval[i]) - as.numeric(nwss_wval[i]))
    })
    
    cat("Discordance Category Distance Distribution:\n")
    print(table(discordance_distance))
  }
  
  # Return the comparison table invisibly
  invisible(comparison_df)
}
























# ============================================================
# EXPORT RESULTS TO CSV FILES
# ============================================================
# Goal:
# Save site, county, and state WVAL outputs as CSV files
#
# Files will be saved to your current working directory
# (use getwd() to check where that is)
# ============================================================


# -----------------------------
# SITE-LEVEL OUTPUTS
# -----------------------------
write.csv(site_ag_sars, "site_wval_sars.csv", row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(site_ag_flu,  "site_wval_flu.csv",  row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(site_ag_rsv,  "site_wval_rsv.csv",  row.names = FALSE)


# -----------------------------
# COUNTY-LEVEL OUTPUTS
# -----------------------------
write.csv(county_ag_sars, "county_wval_sars.csv", row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(county_ag_flu,  "county_wval_flu.csv",  row.names = FALSE)
# Optional:
# write.csv(county_ag_rsv, "county_wval_rsv.csv", row.names = FALSE)


# -----------------------------
# STATE-LEVEL OUTPUTS
# -----------------------------
write.csv(state_wval_sarscov2, "state_wval_sars.csv", row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(state_wval_flu,      "state_wval_flu.csv",  row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(state_wval_rsv,      "state_wval_rsv.csv",  row.names = FALSE)


# -----------------------------
# OPTIONAL: INTERMEDIATE OUTPUTS
# -----------------------------
# These are useful for debugging or deeper analysis

# write.csv(unaggregated_result, "sample_level_wval.csv", row.names = FALSE)
# write.csv(intermediate_result, "intermediate_wval.csv", row.names = FALSE)

```
